([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Seleção Brasileira de Futebol Sub-23*) represents [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") in international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") competitions during [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") and [Pan American Games](/wiki/Pan_American_Games "Pan American Games"). The selection is limited to players under the age of 23, except three overage players. The team is controlled by the [Brazilian Football Confederation](/wiki/Brazilian_Football_Confederation "Brazilian Football Confederation") (CBF). Brazil U23 is one of the most successful teams in the [Olympic football tournament](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics"), having won it twice ([2016](/wiki/Football_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament") and [2020](/wiki/Football_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament")) and securing a record total of seven medals, including two golds, three silvers, and two bronzes.

The **Brazil national under-23 football team** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Seleção Brasileira de Futebol Sub-23*) represents [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") in international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") competitions during [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") and [Pan American Games](/wiki/Pan_American_Games "Pan American Games"). The selection is limited to players under the age of 23, except three overage players. The team is controlled by the [Brazilian Football Confederation](/wiki/Brazilian_Football_Confederation "Brazilian Football Confederation") (CBF). Brazil U23 is one of the most successful teams in the [Olympic football tournament](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics"), having won it twice ([2016](/wiki/Football_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament") and [2020](/wiki/Football_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament")) and securing a record total of seven medals, including two golds, three silvers, and two bronzes.

The [Olympic football tournament](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics") was the last international competition in football organized by FIFA which Brazil had never won until they won at home in [2016](/wiki/Football_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 2016 Summer Olympics"). They had previously won three silver medals ([1984](/wiki/Football_at_the_1984_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1984 Summer Olympics"), [1988](/wiki/Football_at_the_1988_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1988 Summer Olympics"), [2012](/wiki/Football_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2012 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament")) and two bronze medals ([1996](/wiki/Football_at_the_1996_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 1996 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament"), [2008](/wiki/Football_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2008 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament")).[[1]](#cite_note-Olympics_1992--1) The team was often coached by the in-charge [senior team](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team") coach in the past, such as [Mário Zagallo](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Zagallo "Mário Zagallo") in 1996, [Vanderlei Luxemburgo](/wiki/Vanderlei_Luxemburgo "Vanderlei Luxemburgo") in 2000, [Dunga](/wiki/Dunga "Dunga") in 2008 and [Mano Menezes](/wiki/Mano_Menezes "Mano Menezes") in 2012.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1952–1976 Summer Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1952–1976 Summer Olympics")]

Brazil's first participation in the Olympics was in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), in 1952. In that year, Brazil reached the quarter-finals, when they were eliminated by [West Germany](/wiki/Germany_Olympic_football_team "Germany Olympic football team") 4–2.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 1960, in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"),[[3]](#cite_note-rome1960-3) in 1964 in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan,[[4]](#cite_note-tokyo1964-4) in 1968 in [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"),[[5]](#cite_note-mexicocity1968-5) and in 1972 in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"),[[6]](#cite_note-berlin1972-6) Brazil was eliminated in the first stage. In [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), 1976, Brazil was defeated by Poland 2–0 in the semi-finals, then Brazil was defeated by the Soviet Union 2–0 in the bronze medal match, finishing in the fourth place.[[7]](#cite_note-montreal1976-7) In these six participations, Brazil was represented by a team of junior or non-professional players as the Olympics did not allow professional players to participate during this period, all while state-sponsored communist players were allowed to compete.

### 1984 Summer Olympics – Los Angeles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1984 Summer Olympics – Los Angeles")]

Starting in 1984, professional players were allowed to participate. However, European and South American teams, as traditional football powerhouses that won every single FIFA World Cup, were restricted to players with no more than five "A" caps at the start of the tournament. Brazil won its first medal in 1984, in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), United States. In the group stage, Brazil beat Saudi Arabia 3–1, West Germany 1–0 and Morocco 2–0. In the quarter-finals Brazil defeated Canada in the penalty shootout, then they beat Italy 2–1 after extra-time in the semi-finals, but was beaten by France 2–0 in the gold medal Match, thus winning the silver medal.[[8]](#cite_note-losangeles1984-8)

### 1988 Summer Olympics – Seoul

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1988 Summer Olympics – Seoul")]

The second Brazilian silver medal was won in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), in 1988. Brazil won the medal after defeating in the group stage Nigeria 4–0, Australia 3–0 and Yugoslavia 2–1. In the quarter-finals Brazil beat their South American rivals [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_Olympic_football_team "Argentina Olympic football team") 1–0, then defeated West Germany in the penalty shootout, but was defeated by the Soviet Union 2–1 after extra time in the gold medal match.[[9]](#cite_note-seoul1988-9) [Romário](/wiki/Rom%C3%A1rio "Romário") was the competition's top goal scorer with seven goals.[[10]](#cite_note-seoul1988romario-10)

### 1996 Summer Olympics – Atlanta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1996 Summer Olympics – Atlanta")]

Starting in 1992, only players under the age of 23 were allowed to participate, with an exception of three overage players in the team. Brazil, managed by senior team coach, [Mário Zagallo](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Zagallo "Mário Zagallo"), won the bronze medal for the first time in 1996, in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta,_Georgia "Atlanta, Georgia"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). In the group stage, Brazil was beaten by Japan 1–0 in the first match, then they beat Hungary 3–1 and Nigeria 1–0, finishing in the group's first position. After beating Ghana 4–2 in the quarter-finals, Brazil was defeated by Nigeria 4–3 after extra time. In the bronze medal match, Brazil beat Portugal 5–0.[[11]](#cite_note-atlanta1996-11)

### 2000 Summer Olympics – Sydney

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 Summer Olympics – Sydney")]

Brazil, managed by senior team coach, [Vanderlei Luxemburgo](/wiki/Vanderlei_Luxemburgo "Vanderlei Luxemburgo"), was eliminated in the quarter-finals. In the group stage, Brazil beat by Slovakia 3–1 in the first match, then they were beaten by South Africa 3–1. In the last group match, Brazil beat Japan 1–0 to secure the first position in the group stage. In the quarter-finals, Brazil was beaten by Cameroon 1–2, who later won the gold medal.[[12]](#cite_note-sydney2000-12)

### 2003 CONCACAF Gold Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2003 CONCACAF Gold Cup")]

In December 2002, CBF appointed [Ricardo Gomes](/wiki/Ricardo_Gomes "Ricardo Gomes") as the coach for the Brazil Olympic team who were preparing for the [2004 Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 2004 Summer Olympics"). Prior to Olympic qualifying, the Brazil Olympic team or Brazil U23 was sent to compete at the [2003 CONCACAF Gold Cup](/wiki/2003_CONCACAF_Gold_Cup "2003 CONCACAF Gold Cup"). Brazil was invited to the tournament and decided to send their Under-23 team, due to their senior team competing a month earlier at the [2003 FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/2003_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2003 FIFA Confederations Cup"). Although Brazil competed with an U23 team, all the appearances and goals in this tournament were recognized by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") as full international [caps](/wiki/Cap_(sport) "Cap (sport)").[[13]](#cite_note-13) The Brazil U23 team advanced all the way to the final, but were defeated by [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico_national_football_team "Mexico national football team") 0–1 after extra time, denying Brazil the chance to be the first guest team to win the tournament. The following year Brazil failed to qualify for the 2004 Olympic Games after losing out to Paraguay and Argentina in the [qualifying tournament](/wiki/2004_CONMEBOL_Pre-Olympic_Tournament "2004 CONMEBOL Pre-Olympic Tournament").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### 2008 Summer Olympics – Beijing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2008 Summer Olympics – Beijing")]

Brazil, managed by senior team coach, [Dunga](/wiki/Dunga "Dunga"), finished in the first position in the group stage, ahead of Belgium, New Zealand and China, which they beat 1–0, 5–0 and 3–0 respectively.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In the second round, Brazil beat Cameroon 2–0 after extra time.[[16]](#cite_note-cameroon2008-16) Brazil and [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_Olympic_football_team "Argentina Olympic football team") met on August 19 in the semi-final game of the competition. The game was marred by numerous fouls and two ejections for Brazil. Argentina won 3–0.[[17]](#cite_note-argentina2008-17) In the bronze medal match, Brazil beat Belgium 3–0.[[18]](#cite_note-belgium2008-18)

### 2012 Summer Olympics – London

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2012 Summer Olympics – London")]

Brazil, under coach [Mano Menezes](/wiki/Mano_Menezes "Mano Menezes"), was defeated by Mexico 2–1 in the gold medal match, played on 11 August,[[19]](#cite_note-19) after beating Egypt, Belarus and New Zealand in the preliminary round, Honduras in the quarter-finals and South Korea in the semi-finals. Before the Games, they beat Great Britain 2–0 in a friendly game.

### 2016 Summer Olympics – Rio de Janeiro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2016 Summer Olympics – Rio de Janeiro")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Brazil_vs_Honduras%2C_men%27s_football_tournament_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics%2C_Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium%2C_Rio_de_Janeiro%2C_Brazil_%282%29.jpg/250px-Brazil_vs_Honduras%2C_men%27s_football_tournament_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics%2C_Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium%2C_Rio_de_Janeiro%2C_Brazil_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brazil_vs_Honduras,_men%27s_football_tournament_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics,_Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium,_Rio_de_Janeiro,_Brazil_(2).jpg)

Brazil vs Honduras during the men's football tournament at the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics").

Brazil finished in the first position in the group stage, ahead of Denmark (won 4–0), Iraq (tied 0–0) and South Africa (tied 0–0), with the two latter games were a slumpy start for Brazil. In the second round, Brazil beat Colombia 2–0 and in the semi-final match, Brazil played a one-sided game against Honduras and won 6–0. In the final against Germany, on 20 August 2016 – the first match between the two teams in any FIFA-sanctioned tournament since the historic [2014 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup") [semi-final](/wiki/Brazil_v_Germany_(2014_FIFA_World_Cup) "Brazil v Germany (2014 FIFA World Cup)") – Brazil edged a 5–4 victory on penalties after a 1–1 draw. [Neymar](/wiki/Neymar "Neymar"), captaining the side, scored the decisive penalty to win the tournament for the first time ever.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### 2020 Summer Olympics – Tokyo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazil_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020 Summer Olympics – Tokyo")]

Brazil qualified for the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament") as the runners-up, of the [2020 CONMEBOL Pre-Olympic Tournament](/wiki/2020_CONMEBOL_Pre-Olympic_Tournament "2020 CONMEBOL Pre-Olympic Tournament") in a rather difficult campaign. The team finished at the top of their group with 7 points, following a 4–2 win over [Germany](/wiki/Germany_Olympic_football_team "Germany Olympic football team"), a 0–0 draw to [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_national_under-23_football_team "Ivory Coast national under-23 football team") and a 3–1 win over [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_under-23_football_team "Saudi Arabia national under-23 football team"). They beat [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_national_under-23_football_team "Egypt national under-23 football team") 1–0 in the quarter-finals, and [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico_national_Olympic_football_team "Mexico national Olympic football team") in the semi-finals with a 4–1 victory in the penalty shootouts following a 0–0 draw in extra time. In the [final](/wiki/Football_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament_%E2%80%93_Final "Football at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament – Final") against [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_under-23_football_team "Spain national under-23 football team"), [Matheus Cunha](/wiki/Matheus_Cunha "Matheus Cunha") opened the score for Brazil in the first half and a [Mikel Oyarzabal](/wiki/Mikel_Oyarzabal "Mikel Oyarzabal") goal in the second half forced the match into extra time; [Malcom](/wiki/Malcom_(footballer) "Malcom (footballer)") scored the winning goal in the 108th minute, which lead Brazil to their second Olympic gold medal, consecutively after their first win in Rio five years prior.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)
